speaker: interviewer
text: January 11, 1996. So what's your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Ester, middle name is Theresa. And her maiden name is W-I-D-R-I-C-H, Widrich. The Hungarian crew, or the old crew call her Terry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And her Israeli crew call her Etti.

speaker: interviewer
text: Etti.

speaker: interviewee
text: Short for Ester. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we met. And we fell in love, and we got married in 1966. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She stopped working a couple of months later. She was working for El Al. And I, then I was drafted in the Army during Vietnam. And we were in the Army together. She had been in the Army before, in Israel, in the Air force, as a matter of fact. And we lived in Pennsylvania, and then we came back. I finished my residency. And we've been living here in New York, since I came back from the Army in '68. We have a daughter. Her name is Daniela. And she was born on July of 1975.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have anything to say about the experience, [BACKGROUND NOISE] about your wartime experience and how, what kind of impact you think it may have had on your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Never forget. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You can forgive some things, some things you can't forgive. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And unfortunately, things always repeat, no matter what you do. And if you just take a look at what's going on now in my former country, former Yugoslavia. You know, my passport changed from Yugoslav to Serbian-- not my passport, but my place of birth changed from Yugoslavia to Serbia. And the people are still at each other the way they were before. The Aryan nation, these white supremacist groups. It can happen anyplace, anytime. And it's unfortunate. Because this is human nature. And as enlightened as people are, they're never enlightened enough. I mean, you would not think that Germany was a backward, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] religious, fundamentalistic country. I mean, sometimes you associate these hatreds with fundamental Islam like that have currently with the Shiites and the Hamas fundamentalists and the fundamentalists in Algeria and what have you. Or that you know, if they had the Inquisition under the tutelage of the church, in a very educated and a very liberal and open society like Germany, that happened. So it can happen in the United States. Forewarned is forearmed. The more people that know about this, the more people will be on guard. And you know, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there are a lot of things that I don't believe in. And I don't believe that the American Civil Liberties Union, with all their Jewish lawyers, should go and defend the Nazis. Let them get their own Nazi lawyers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think that a lot of times Jews are putting to fault their own. What will people think? Let them think what they will. And if I do this, they will not do this to me. So they are working from an inferiority complex, a so-called Jewish guilt, which I don't have.

speaker: interviewee
text: One nice thing about the war experience was that when I was drafted into the war in Vietnam, and people were running away, and I really despise those people. Every Jew would have wanted to be a Hungarian or a Frenchman or a German and be able to fight for this country. But they weren't, and they were put in concentration camps. Here you have Jews who are Americans, who go through school, use everything that this country has to offer. And then comes a war. It is not the 18-year-olds who decide foreign policy. It's the government decides foreign policy in a democracy. And then, they don't take up their obligation.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't have that in Israel. Israeli kids don't run away. So when I got drafted I said, hey, I'm an American, it is my duty and obligation. I'm going to serve. And you know what? If that SOB, Hitler and Eichmann couldn't kill me, a couple of Vietnamese are not going to kill me. So I have sort of an outlook, if the-- Germans couldn't kill me, there was a reason for me not to be killed by the Germans, to have survived the war, and to live a long and healthy life. I think it's a good outlook. I'm not bitter. Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: That was really good.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photograph of my maternal grandfather, Mr. Goldner, in his Army uniform. This was during World War I, approximately 1916. He is at home on furlough. He was in the Quartermasters Corps. And the little girl in the white dress and the white hat is my mother, Anna. This is a Berger family memorial stone. And on top of it is my grandfather's name. In the middle is my uncle's name, my father's older brother, after whom I was named. And it shows where they died, when they died. And the last name on the bottom is my paternal grandmother, who died in Bergen-Belsen. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] This is in Yugoslavia. This is a-- end of 1937 or early 1938, family portrait. And from left to right is my sister, Myriam, sitting on my father's lap. Immediately to his right is my maternal grandmother, Regina Goldner. Standing is my late uncle. His name was Imre Goldner. Next to him is his father, Mr. Goldner; my mother's youngest brother, Tibor Goldner. The lady seated next to him was my father's mother, my maternal grandmother. Next to him is my mother's next brother, Emil, and my mother. And then it's me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think it's the end of 1937. It must have been two or three months, maybe early 1938. I must have been 3 months old at the time. This is a photograph taken in spring of 1945, several months after coming out of concentration camp. This is in Switzerland. It's on the step of the little hotel that was the hospital. Pictured are my parents, my sister and I. We don't look so bad, but my parents, you can see, still see how gaunt they are. This is about four or five months after we were in Switzerland. I think it was the occasion of the peace with the Germans in the spring of '45. This is June, 1947. We are on our way to the United States. Pictured here are my sister and I at the railway station in Montreux, just before we boarded the train to go to Basel. And then from Basel we went to London and then to the USA. This is fourth or fifth day out. Must have been either the 10th or the 11th or maybe the 12th of July, 1947, on the ship MS Batory in the Atlantic, heading west to the promised land. Not Israel, but the promised land then was the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said it wasn't-- there was no Israel then, so the promised land was the United States in 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my father and my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sometime, 12th or 13th of July, 1947, on the ship MS Batory. The young man is me, looking out, thinking of the future. I was married December 1, 1966, at City Hall in New York, with    a civil ceremony. When we had the opportunity to have a real religious wedding, it was held at the Shulamit Hotel in Haifa, in May of 1967. And here pictured is the bridegroom, the bride, and the mother of the bridegroom. July 17, 1975, the next generation came to pass. This a picture of my daughter, Daniela with her grandmother, at about three weeks of age. 1992, Tel Aviv, my daughter, Daniela and her cat. In the background is her mother and her grand uncle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please introduce your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- I'd like to introduce my family. Sitting next to me is my wife, Ester. And standing behind us is our lovely daughter, Daniela. Daniela's a college student. She's attending school at Ithaca. And my wife and I are getting old together, here in New York, for the moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Older.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you  have anything you would like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. It's just been-- we know each other for very many years, as you must have heard before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Since you were a baby. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Just about. And the families are connected, which is very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's all. Everything else is fine. We're happy to be together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thanks.
